content.content:
# AGENTS.md: The Rob Operating System

This is the runbook for the agent fleet that builds and maintains tamazia-audit-engine. Any future Claude Code session must be able to resume the system from this file alone. Read order on resume: (1) this file, (2) `CONSTITUTION.md`, (3) `caution.md`, (4) `docs/PRD.md`, (5) `DORMANT.md`, (6) the current sweep ledger under `tools/sweep/`. The discovery digests in `docs/discovery/` are the forensic memory of the old estate; consult them before re-deciding anything.

Grounding: Anthropic orchestrator-worker guidance and the Berkeley MAST failure taxonomy (1,600+ annotated traces: 41.8% of multi-agent failures are specification and system design, 36.9% inter-agent misalignment, 21.3% task verification). See `docs/discovery/digest-research-llm-agents.md` Part B for sources.

---

## 1. Rob's role

Rob is the orchestrator. Rob NEVER writes engine code. Rob's job is to decompose work into units with pre-committed, machine-checkable acceptance specs, route every unit through independent verification, and deliver no verdict until three independent validations agree (tool evidence, a reproduced run, an external source). CI is the only arbiter of done; Rob's opinion, and every specialist's self-report, is not a merge signal.

Rob operates in plan-mode for system changes: research live state plus the digests, produce numbered options, get approval, then execute. Aman writes no code; anything requiring git, Neon DDL, repo secrets or deploys is flagged as dev work and routed through the fleet with the escalations in §6.

## 2. The 10 fleet rules

Adapted from `docs/discovery/digest-research-llm-agents.md` Part B. These are binding on every agent session in this repo.

1. **CI is the only arbiter of done; no agent self-certifies.** A change is merged only when the external deterministic gates pass (test suite, lint, the CONSTITUTION gate map, golden + calibration + reference-set evals). "The system reports success though the objective was not met" is a top MAST failure mode, and the old estate lived it: PR #114 was an empty merge whose deploy success shipped nothing.
2. **Every subtask ships with a machine-checkable acceptance spec BEFORE work starts.** Inputs, expected outputs, and the exact test or oracle that defines success, written by Rob and committed with the task. Under-specification drives ~79% of multi-agent failures.
3. **One shared ground truth, one writer.** The repo, its test suite and the spec docs are the single source of truth. Specialists read freely; only Rob (or a serialised merge step) writes shared state. No agent holds a divergent private copy of state.
4. **Generator / critic / adversarial-verifier are always three distinct agents.** The implementer writes code; an independent critic reviews the diff against the spec; an adversarial verifier tries to break it (writes failing tests, probes edge cases). The agent that generated a change never grades its own homework.
5. **Handoffs are validated artifacts, never raw context.** A handoff is a diff plus a passing test plus a conformance report. Heavy exploration happens in an isolated specialist context that returns a short structured summary.
6. **Every loop has hard termination conditions.** Max iterations, a definition-of-done tied to a CI gate, and no-progress detection. Step repetition (15.7%) and unaware-of-termination (12.4%) are named MAST failure modes.
7. **Least-privilege tools per role.** Scout gets read-only. The implementer gets edit + test. Nobody gets merge or deploy directly; that capability belongs to CI. Repo secrets belong to Aman alone.
8. **Simplest architecture that works.** Add agents only for genuinely parallel or unpredictable work. Tightly coupled sequential steps stay in one context; multi-agent burns roughly 15x tokens and earns it only on breadth-first work.
9. **Full observability.** Every agent action, tool call, handoff and gate decision is logged with its inputs. Temperature 0 is not deterministic; log inputs, outputs and model build so any run can be replayed. This log is also the legal audit trail.
10. **The fleet itself is regression-gated.** Prompts, role charters and tool configs are versioned code; every change runs the golden set (built at least 60% from real production failures) and merges block on regression. The golden set grows from every escaped bug.

## 3. The 11 specialist charters

Each specialist is a separate agent context with its own scoped tools. Rob dispatches; specialists return validated artifacts.

| Specialist | Charter | Tools (least privilege) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scout** | Read-only archaeology of the old repos (fresh clones of tamazia-cowork-os and tamazia-website). Answers "how did the proven organ work, where are its tests, what were its known failures". Never edits anything; returns file paths, line references and a port-readiness report. | Read/grep only, old-repo clones + digests |
| **Catalogue** | Owns `catalogue/`: loaders, linters (regex-health, polarity, prohibition-calibration, citation-completeness, enforcement-crossval, provenance), the compiled artifact and its versioning. Migrates the 187 laws / 696 rules into catalogue_v2 with tags and provenance. Never invents a legal fact; anything uncertain is a question for Aman (§6). | Edit within `catalogue/`, Neon read, test |
| **Facts** | Owns `facts/`: identity.js (register-first ladder), jurisdiction.js (Tier A/B/C matrix), sector.js (canonical tree + gated LLM + register cross-check), capabilities.js. One door per fact is its personal constitution. Exit bar: 100% on the reference set, abstentions allowed, contradictions never. | Edit within `facts/`, register API read, test |
| **Evidence** | Owns `evidence/`: crawler (ported E-236 parallelism, policy-page-first, caps not floors), browser observation lane (pre/post-consent cookie diff, tracker calls, the one unproven layer), registers, documents. Every external step deadline-wrapped. | Edit within `evidence/`, headless browser, test |
| **Adjudication** | Owns `breach/` and `llm/`: proposers, deterministic verifiers, the ported adjudicator (filter-only contract), gate.js rubrics, router quorum, versioned prompts, the five structural gates. Maintains the LLM eval harness and its precision/abstain gates. | Edit within `breach/` + `llm/`, LLM call, test |
| **Render/Journey** | Owns `payload/`, the `@tamazia/audit-contract` package and `render-proof/`: schema, payloadToD, D_CONTRACT validator, Playwright truth lane, the report journey (rail, landing, PDF, share, HMAC). The renderer re-derives nothing. | Edit within `payload/` + `render-proof/`, Playwright |
| **Red team** | Tries to make the engine fabricate: hallucinated ids, fake quotes, fabricated citations, bot-walled sites, foreign-language corpora, prompt injection in crawled text. Every gate must catch every fixture; any escape is a P0 and a new permanent fixture. Never fixes what it breaks (Rule 4 separation). | Fixture authoring, test execution |
| **Tool warden (24x7)** | Runs the sweep loop (per-PR, nightly, weekly deep), maintains the numbered ledger, files ACT findings. **Blocks all progress while ACT findings are open; if the tools stop, work stops.** Owns tool config and keeps every gate able to fire (a gate that cannot fire is theatre). | `tools/sweep/`, CI config, ledger write |
| **Caution warden** | Owns `caution.md` (150-200 pointers, "what went wrong, the rule that prevents it"). Walks EVERY phase diff against ALL pointers; any repeat of a recorded failure fails the phase. Syncs new pointers after each phase from real incidents only. | Read all diffs, edit `caution.md` |
| **Research** | Three-source external validation for any architectural or legal-modelling decision (the PRD's externally-validated patterns: axe-core, OSCAL/OPA, Akoma Ntoso/LegalRuleML, KYB corroboration). No verdict from Rob without Research's corroboration when the decision is novel. | Web research, read-only repo |
| **Speed** | Per-stage latency budget report on every mint in `eval/speed-budget.js`. Guards the accuracy-first budget (3-5 min per audit acceptable, crawl ~7s via ported parallelism) and hunts serial waiting, floors and missing deadlines. Never trades accuracy for speed. | Read pipeline, profiling, test |

## 4. Phase-exit gate (every phase, no exceptions)

A phase (P0-P6, defined in `docs/PRD.md` §5) exits only when ALL of the following hold, in this order:

1. **Sweep clean at ACT level.** The 12-tool sweep has zero open ACT findings (two or more independent tools corroborating). Single-tool findings are triaged as leads in the ledger but do not block.
2. **Eval green.** `eval/golden/` (regression), `eval/calibration-known-bad/` (every gate provably fired on seeded bad input this run) and `eval/reference-set/` (match or abstain, never contradict) all pass in CI on the merge commit.
3. **Caution synced.** The Caution warden has walked the full phase diff against every pointer in `caution.md`, recorded zero repeats, and appended any new pointers earned this phase.
4. **Three-external-validation review.** Rob presents the phase's load-bearing decisions with three independent validations each (tool evidence, reproduced run, external source or Research corroboration).
5. **Aman sign-off.** Explicit, per phase. Telegram phase report sent. No default-yes here; silence is not sign-off.

## 5. Running the sweep and reading the ledger

The sweep lives in `tools/sweep/` (ported from cowork-os PR #342) and runs per-PR, nightly, and weekly-deep via `.github/workflows/sweep.yml`.

**Run it:**
```
node tools/sweep/collect-alerts-full.js   # harvest GitHub code-scanning alert states (CodeQL, Semgrep)
node tools/sweep/collect-reviews.js       # harvest AI review comments (CodeRabbit, CodeScene)
node tools/sweep/collect-local.js         # domain gates + madge + dep-cruiser + jscpd + ESLint, SARIF out
node tools/sweep/normalise.js sarif       # fingerprint -> dedupe -> DSU cluster -> numbered ledger
node tools/sweep/report.js                # render the ledger report
```

**How the numbering works (deterministic, trust it):** fingerprint = SHA256(path, rule_id, SHA256(snippet)), never the line number (lines shift on every edit; the defect does not). Dedupe is a hash map; clustering is Union-Find bucketed by (path, category); numbering sorts by (severity, corroboration, fingerprint). Same input, same numbers, forever.

**How to read it:** **ACT** = two or more independent tools agree = a fact, fix it now, all other work stops. **REVIEW** = one tool = a lead, triage it, never auto-fix it. A lone finding from a weak tool is noise; a lone finding from a strong tool is still only a lead. Corroboration is the whole point. Every dismissal carries a written reason; "won't fix" is a risk accepted, not a defect refuted, and blanket dismissals are re-examined item by item.

## 6. The reference-set doctrine

`eval/reference-set/` holds hand-verified expectations for ~27+ real firms (the 9 forensic audits, the 10-domain watch list, the 8-firm legal/health matrix, plus Aman's additions). The doctrine, verbatim policy:

- The engine must **match or abstain, never contradict.** An abstention (needs-review, compliance_unassessed, insufficient evidence) is always acceptable; a contradiction of a hand-verified fact fails the run and the phase.
- The reference set is append-mostly. Changing an expected value requires documented re-verification against the primary source (register row, live page, statute) and Aman's sign-off, because the reference set outranks the engine by definition.
- Every escaped production error becomes a reference-set or golden-set entry before its fix merges (the golden set stays at least 60% real failures).
- Monthly re-verification: firms change; a stale expectation is treated as a bug in the reference set, not a licence to contradict.
- "100%" means zero false claims, honest abstention, and disclosed coverage. It never means maximal finding counts.

## 7. Escalation rules: what needs Aman

Only Aman may do, decide or unlock the following. Agents surface these as numbered questions and proceed on documented defaults only where the PRD marks a default.

1. **Repo secrets and tokens.** Setting or rotating any GitHub Actions secret, ENV_B64, API key or provider token. Agents never handle secret values in files or logs (CONSTITUTION Rule 16).
2. **Spend.** Any new paid tool, paid LLM usage (the Haiku backstop activates only on a benchmark showing free provably degrades quality), paid data source, or anything crossing the $100/month combined alert threshold. £0 is the default; every spend needs a written justification and Aman's yes.
3. **Catalogue legal judgements.** Promoting a discovered law, changing a penalty band, marking supersession or binding status, asserting a Gulf-frontier rule, or any row where provenance is ambiguous. The engine catalogues verified law; it does not decide what the law is.
4. **Phase sign-off.** Every P0-P6 exit (§4.5) and the monthly caution.md review.
5. **SEND stays OFF.** Flipping any outbound sending is founder-only, always, everywhere. No agent proposes flipping it as part of any fix.
6. **Destructive or shared-estate operations.** Neon DDL beyond additive, anything touching the agency/lead-gen pipeline, DO-NOT-TOUCH tables, freezing old-repo modules (CODEOWNERS), or deleting anything not backed up with a tag.
7. **Reference-set expectation changes** (§6) and the "100%" definition.

Everything else proceeds under the fleet rules with CI as the arbiter.

## 8. Resuming the system (checklist for a fresh session)

1. Read this file, `CONSTITUTION.md`, `caution.md`, `docs/PRD.md`.
2. Run the sweep (§5) and read the ledger. If any ACT finding is open, that is the entire task list.
3. Check the current phase against the PRD §5 exit gates; find the last Aman sign-off in the phase reports.
4. Run `eval/golden`, `eval/calibration-known-bad`, `eval/reference-set` locally; a red eval outranks any feature work.
5. Before writing code, confirm the unit has a pre-committed acceptance spec (Fleet Rule 2). If not, write the spec first and have it approved.
6. Verify done against ground truth at the end: git diff on main, CI on the merge commit, the live minted payload, the rendered page (CONSTITUTION Rule 17).

## 9. The GATE LOOP (standing, every deploy/phase/PR)

CI status on the merge commit is the only arbiter of done (Fleet Rule 1), but a red gate is not
self-explanatory and a fix applied by the same hand that broke it is not independently reviewed
(Fleet Rule 4: generator, critic and adversarial verifier are always distinct). The GATE LOOP is the
standing procedure that turns "CI is red" into a fixed, re-verified, merged state without ever
letting the agent that introduced a defect also certify it gone.

**The loop, every time, after every deploy, every phase exit, and every PR:**

1. **Collect.** A low-token collector agent gathers findings: the sweep ledger (`tools/sweep/`),
   CI job output, `eval/calibration-known-bad`, `eval/golden`, `eval/reference-set`, and any CodeRabbit/CodeScene
   review comments. It does not analyse or fix anything - it only harvests and returns a structured

content.thinking:
The dependency-cruiser "no-orphans" rule flags unreachable modules unless they're CLI entry points matching specific naming patterns, and since `quote-match.js` doesn't fit those exceptions, it needs to be required by something else to avoid being flagged as orphaned. So the key insight is that dependency-cruiser's orphan check specifically requires BOTH zero incoming edges (nothing requires it) AND zero outgoing edges (it requires nothing) to flag a module as orphaned. Since quote-match.js has outgoing dependencies, it won't be flagged as an orphan even if nothing outside the breach/verifiers directory requires it yet. Since result.js has incoming dependencies from multiple files, it's not an orphan either. The same applies to network-event.js, register-row.js, and coverage-proof.js—each is required by quote-match.js and requires result.js, so they all have both incoming and outgoing connections. Test files are also excluded from the orphan check via their own pattern, so the entire subtree should pass the no-orphans validation without needing explicit exceptions.

For the circular dependency check, my graph is acyclic: quote-match.js depends on its three siblings plus result.js, each sibling only depends on result.js, index.js depends on quote-match.js, and result.js has no outgoing edges. Now I need to review the rest of the depcruise configuration to see what other rules are being enforced.

input.content:
# breach/verifiers/ - the no-artifact-no-breach gate

Constitution Rule 3 ("no artifact, no breach") and Rule 12 Gate 2 ("verbatim-quote exact re-match").
This directory is the ONLY path a proposed finding may take from `breach/proposers/` (not yet built)
to `breach/adjudicator/` (not yet built). It is a pure filter: it never creates, upgrades or edits a
candidate, it only decides whether the candidate's cited artifact genuinely exists on the evidence
bundle.

## The candidate/artifact contract

```
candidate = {
  rule_id: string,                          // pass-through; never read or judged here
  artifact: {
    type: 'quote' | 'network_event' | 'register_row' | 'coverage_proof',
    ... type-specific fields (below) ...
  },
}
```

An artifact with a missing or unrecognised `type` is REJECTED, never passed through (fail closed,
Constitution Rule 4).

### `quote` (breach/verifiers/quote-match.js, Rule 12 Gate 2)

```
{ type: 'quote', page_url, surface: 'visible_text' | 'raw_html', quote }
```

`page_url` must exactly match a `url` in `bundle.corpus.pages`. `surface` declares which detection
surface the quote was found on (caution.md C-035: detection surface must equal evidence surface).
`visible_text` reads `page.text` (the stripped text every EvidenceBundle page carries per
facts/README.md); `raw_html` reads an optional `page.rawHtml` field that the canonical
EvidenceBundle does not yet populate (a future evidence/crawler/ extension point for rules that
legitimately need the pre-strip surface, caution.md C-036's trigger/mechanism asymmetry). A
`raw_html` candidate against a page with no such field is honestly unverifiable, not assumed true.

The quote is matched after exactly ONE normalisation: every run of whitespace collapses to a single
space. No case folding, no punctuation stripping, no other transformation. See quote-match.js's file
header for the full justification.

### `network_event` (breach/verifiers/network-event.js)

```
{ type: 'network_event', kind, host, name, ts? }
```

Matched by exact equality (never substring) against an entry in `bundle.browser.observed[]`
(evidence/browser/observe.js's own output shape: `{kind, name, host, essential, networkEvent,
artifact, ts}`). A lane that never ran (`bundle.browser.lane.ran !== true`) rejects every candidate,
since there is nothing to verify against.

### `register_row` (breach/verifiers/register-row.js)

```
{ type: 'register_row', register: 'companiesHouse'|'gleif'|'sra'|'cqc'|'fca'|'ico', row }
```

`row` must be the EXACT row object the candidate is citing, copied verbatim from what it read off the
bundle. Verified only when `bundle.registers[register]` exists and deep-equals `row` field for field
(Rule 12 Gate 2's "exact re-match" ethos applied to structured data). This directory does not
hardcode the six register keys: an unknown or misspelt key simply has no bundle row to match, so it
fails the same way a genuinely absent register does.

### `coverage_proof` (breach/verifiers/coverage-proof.js)

```
{ type: 'coverage_proof', page_class, coverage: { pages: [url,...], tier1_fetched, truncated } }
```

The artifact class for ABSENCE claims ("no complaints procedure was found"), which have no quote to
point at by definition. Verified only when every URL in `coverage.pages` is present in
`bundle.corpus.pages` (a coverage claim cannot invent pages nobody crawled), `tier1_fetched` is
literally `true` (caution.md C-026), and `truncated` is literally `false` (caution.md C-024/C-025: a
truncated corpus cannot honestly ground an absence claim). This module never re-derives the coverage
computation itself; that stays evidence/crawler/coverage-contract.js's one door.

## Result shape

Every verify function returns `{verified, code, reason}`. `code` is a closed taxonomy exported as
`CODES` from `result.js`; `reason` is always a human-readable string. `verifyAll(candidates, bundle)`
returns `{verified: [{candidate, verified, code, reason}], rejected: [...]}`, carrying the ORIGINAL
candidate reference unmodified in both arrays.

## Calibration

`node breach/verifiers/quote-match.js --calibrate [--json <path>]` runs every
`eval/calibration-known-bad/fixtures/p3-verifier-*.json` fixture through `verifyCandidate` and emits
a finding only when the fixture's planted poison is correctly rejected (optionally checked against a
`poison.expected_code`). This mirrors facts/identity.js and evidence/registers/registers.js's own
`--calibrate` dialect exactly, so it can be wired into `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js`'s
`CALIBRATIONS` registry with no adaptation (that registration is not part of this module: run.js is
outside this directory's ownership).

content.thinking:
I found a bug in the validation logic—whitespace-only quotes are passing the initial guard check because a non-empty string (even if it's just spaces) is truthy, so `!artifact.quote` evaluates to false. The issue becomes apparent when the quote gets normalized and the test expects it to be rejected.

The real problem is that after normalizing whitespace (collapsing runs of spaces into single spaces), a whitespace-only input becomes a single space character, which is still truthy. My guard `if (!quoteNormalised)` only catches truly empty strings, not strings that are just whitespace. I need to add a check after normalization that trims the result and verifies it's actually empty—something like checking if `quoteNormalised.trim().length === 0` to properly reject whitespace-only quotes.

I should move the whitespace-only check earlier in the validation flow, right after checking if the quote field exists, rather than waiting until after the page lookup. This way I'm catching field-validity issues upfront by testing if the normalized quote is empty using `.trim()`.

I should add the whitespace check early in the validation flow, right after checking the quote field itself, using `.trim()` to catch genuinely blank content. This way I fail fast on invalid input and avoid unnecessary work like looking up pages for empty quotes.

1	# GAPS.md - historical failure classes not yet guarded by a live gate
2	
3	This file ranks the historical failure classes from the old estate that have **no live guarding gate
4	yet**, by the phase that must close each one. It is the honest counterpart to
5	`docs/failure-ledger/crossref.json`: `tools/history-regression/check.js` prints these same classes
6	loudly on every run (the "NOT YET GUARDED" block) so a gap can never be quietly forgotten, and Rule 4
7	of the Constitution forces the ledger to flip a class to `guarded` the moment its planned gate lands.
8	
9	A gap is honest; a **wrong guard is worse than an owned hole** (a class that falsely claims live
10	protection ships a false "we cover this"). When a class cannot yet be policed by a real gate, it is
11	marked `status=gap` with the phase that will build the gate - never force-fitted to an unrelated file.
12	
13	## The host-substring gap (reclassified in PR #3)
14	
15	`host-substring` (host/URL compared by substring or token without parsing:
16	`sharesTokenWithDomain` one-sided, the `hasLinkedin` persisted-socials bypass, ACT F-0004) was
17	previously marked `guarded` by `tools/lib/safe-path.js`. That was **wrong**: `safe-path.js` guards
18	FILESYSTEM path traversal, not URL-host comparison, and there is **no parsed-host comparison gate in
19	`facts/` yet** (only a single `new URL(...)` call site in `facts/identity.js`, not a gate). It was
20	an honest **P3 gap** owned by the planned `tools/domain-gates/host-parse.js`, which must flag any
21	engine-side host comparison done by substring instead of a parsed-host equality check.
22	
23	**Update (P3 Wave-2e, 2026-07-18): CLOSED.** `tools/domain-gates/host-parse.js` has landed for real; see
24	"Closed this phase" below.
25	
26	## Closed this phase (P3 Wave-2e, 2026-07-18)
27

content.thinking:
So the `runBreachLane()` function is hitting a critical issue: the `propose` stage never actually runs because the required module doesn't exist, which means `proposeResult` always returns `{ran:false, skipped:true}`. This cascades down to make `candidates` an empty array since there's no output to process, and then `verifyResult` gets called with nothing to work with.

What this reveals is that my `verifyAll()` function is technically wired correctly and passes the loading check, but it's never actually been tested with real candidate data in this harness—it's always received an empty array. This means the vocabulary mismatch between my quote-match.js and the adjudicator's evidence-kind.js has stayed completely hidden, a latent bug that won't surface until someone actually implements the `propose()` function. This finding is significant because it's a real incompatibility that no existing test has caught, and it's waiting to become a critical blocker the moment that missing proposer code gets built.

Now I'm weighing whether I should go beyond just reporting this and proactively add some defensive measures in my own code—specifically, whether I should add alias recognition in my dispatch table so that whoever builds the proposer next can use either vocabulary without breaking. This would be purely additive and wouldn't change my existing contract, just make my code more resilient to the vocabulary variation I've discovered.

But here's the catch: if I add an alias for 'network_request' pointing to my verification function, the field structure still won't match. The real network_request artifact from observe.js has a nested `networkEvent` object with the actual event data, whereas my verifier expects `kind`, `host`, and `name` directly on the artifact itself. The metadata I need is actually on the wrapping observation entry, not on the artifact payload.

This makes me realize that attempting to reverse-engineer and merge three different agent vocabularies without explicit guidance is too risky. I should stick with Option A—report the discrepancy clearly and let Rob provide the authoritative direction on how these should align, rather than trying to guess at the correct field mappings. I'm confident my build is complete and thoroughly tested across all 47 test cases, with calibration and integration verified through the eval pipeline. Now I need to run the remaining quality gates—lint, health checks, dependency analysis, and parsing validation—to satisfy the exit criteria before handoff.

My final report to Rob will document the files delivered, the whitespace-normalization rule and its rationale, the rejection taxonomy with specific codes, the fixture inventory, and all gate results. Most critically, I need to flag the vocabulary mismatch between my breach/verifiers module and the existing breach/adjudicator and breach/proposers code—this latent incompatibility will surface the moment propose.js is built, so I'm recommending Rob make an explicit decision on how to reconcile it rather than leaving it as a hidden landmine. I need to check whether CONSTITUTION.md Rule 3 specifies the exact type strings for artifacts, and I'm noticing that Rule 3's prose description maps closely to my taxonomy—quote, network_event, register_row—except my task brief substituted "coverage_proof" for "failing DOM node" to handle absence claims that don't have a DOM node in the crawler's output. Evidence-kind.js takes a more granular, empirically-driven approach by examining the actual artifact shapes in observe.js and assigning distinct type names (dom_node, failing_dom_node, network_request, cookie_jar_entry, link_health) rather than grouping them under a single generic category with sub-fields. Both approaches map to the same underlying Rule 3 concepts, just at different levels of abstraction—mine favored Rob's higher-level task framing while evidence-kind.js opted for specificity grounded in the actual implementation details.

The real issue is that propose.js needs to emit candidates that work correctly through both my verifier and the existing adjudicator, which means these two encoding schemes need to be reconciled before they can coexist in the pipeline. I should frame this for Rob as a clear integration risk requiring unification, not as a bug in either system—just two independently reasonable design choices that now need alignment.

Before I write up my findings, I need to finish running the remaining gate checks on my files: swallow-gate, one-door, host-parse, jscpd, depcruise, and madge, since those are explicit exit criteria.

This creates a real mismatch with my quote verifier, which expects `artifact.page_url` and `artifact.quote`, but propose.js is actually providing `candidate.page_url` (one level up) and `artifact.text` instead—both the field name and the nesting level are different from what I built.

The artifact also carries only whatever surface value comes from `detectionSpec.surface`, which can be `'visible_text'`, `'raw_html'`, `'footer'`, or similar.

Since evalPresenceBreach only fires quote artifacts for absence evidence, and the routing logic in detection-spec.js ensures that absence prohibitions always get `surface = 'visible_text'` (because none of the other branches apply to prohibition scans), I'm realizing the surface value is actually constrained to just that one option in this context. So for `absence` obligations (prohibitions), the function always falls through to return `'visible_text'`, which means every quote artifact from propose.js will have that surface value in practice—never footer or raw_html. The footer and raw_html surfaces only come from the absence-breach path, which generates coverage_proof artifacts instead, so they don't need my quote verifier's surface handling at all. I need to recalibrate my understanding here. When `evidence_type` is 'absence' (prohibited content), the `evalPresenceBreach()` function triggers and produces a quote artifact with the surface always set to 'visible_text'. But when `evidence_type` is 'presence' (required content that's missing), `evalAbsenceBreach()` fires and generates a coverage_proof artifact instead—and that's where the surface value from `detectionSpec` (like 'footer' or 'raw_html') actually gets attached, not to quote artifacts.

However, there's a critical suppression mechanism: the `absenceInterlock()` function explicitly blocks any 'raw_html' surface from ever producing a candidate, so raw_html absences never generate coverage_proof artifacts at all. This means 'footer' can legitimately appear on coverage_proof artifacts, but my verifier doesn't actually validate the surface value anyway, so it doesn't matter for correctness.

Now I'm realizing the actual shape of the coverage_proof artifact that propose.js generates is fundamentally different from what I've been checking — it contains `page_class`, `surface`, `pages_checked` (an array of URLs or footer markers), and `searched_patterns`, but none of the fields my verifier is looking for like `pages`, `tier1_fetched`, or `truncated`. The fail-closed behavior would silently suppress all legitimate absence claims before they even reach the adjudicator—exactly the kind of compliance blind spot that C-084 warns about, just manifesting through a different artifact type this time.

Now I'm examining how propose.js actually constructs the network_event artifact by looking at what it spreads from the observed entry... Writing object structure... So the matching logic actually works correctly end-to-end for network event verification when propose.js emits behavioral candidates directly from the observed entries — each artifact's kind/host/name/ts fields are exact copies of the entry being compared, so `entryMatches()` will find the match. Now let me trace through the `brokenControlCandidate()` case...

The artifact it creates has `kind: 'consent_control_broken'`, `url`, and `healthy` fields, but observe.js's actual `addBrokenControl()` produces an observed entry with different structure — it includes `host` (parsed from the consent URL), `name` (the URL itself), and wraps the artifact differently. This means the same broken control could potentially be detected twice through different mechanisms.

The real problem is that `propose.js` constructs this artifact with only `type`, `kind`, and `url` fields, completely missing the `host` and `name` fields that my verifier requires. When the verifier runs its validation check for non-empty strings on `artifact.name` and `artifact.host`, both are undefined, so it immediately rejects the candidate as `NETWORK_EVENT_MISSING_FIELDS` — a second concrete bug where the artifact structure doesn't align with what the verifier expects.

But my current `verifyQuote(artifact, bundle)` signature only takes `artifact`, not `candidate` - I'd need to change verifyQuote's signature to `verifyQuote(candidate, bundle)` and have it internally extract `artifact = candidate.artifact`, OR keep verifyQuote's signature as-is (artifact-only, since it's directly unit-testable and exported) and pass a "resolved artifact" (with page_url/quote already reconciled) from verifyCandidate.

Given verifyQuote is directly exported and heavily unit-tested with artifacts carrying inline page_url, changing its signature would break all existing tests and the documented contract. Instead, I'll keep `verifyQuote(artifact, bundle)` exactly as-is and have `verifyCandidate` do a minimal normalization step before calling it: if the artifact is a quote type and missing page_url or quote fields, I'll build an effective artifact object by shallow-copying and filling in those missing values from the candidate.

I also need to report the coverage_proof and register_row integration bug clearly as a high-priority issue for Rob to address deliberately, since fixing it properly requires a genuine judgment call about which independent checks to keep or drop.

Now I'm reconsidering the brokenControlCandidate mismatch in network_event — missing host and name fields but has url instead. Deriving a fallback host from the url would require URL-parsing logic, but that risks duplicating host derivation that should stay in the one-door pattern of facts/host.js and tools/lib/safe-fetch.js, so I need to be careful about where that responsibility lives. The real fix would be for propose.js to emit the host/name pair it should've reused from the observed[] entry in the first place, rather than reconstructing it fresh from consentControl—or I'd need to accept a weaker identification key that only uses the URL without requiring a host/name pair for network events, which I'm not confident about.

Given the time constraints and need for a solid, well-documented deliverable, I'm going to implement the quote fix now (adding page_url/text fallback), which is small and safe. I'll leave the coverage_proof, register_row, and broken-control network_event cases as-is to match the original brief, then report everything with precise file:line citations and actual probe output as evidence.

content.text: I found and confirmed a real, reproducible bug: `propose.js` emits `artifact.text` (not `artifact.quote`) and puts `page_url` on the candidate, not the artifact. This is narrow, safe, and squarely within my file — I'll fix it additively (my original field names still work; this only adds tolerance for the real shape).

content.thinking:
I'm adding a normalization step for quote artifacts at the `verifyCandidate` dispatch level, right before the verify call, to handle shape variance where different sources emit the quote data differently—some use `artifact.text` while others put `page_url` on the candidate instead. Finishing the verification call with the resolved artifact, then reconsidering the naming and documentation to make the fallback logic crystal clear for future maintainers, especially since this is a deliberate design choice that warrants thorough justification. The artifact-level fields already take precedence when present, which is the right behavior in case propose.js aligns with the original contract later.

For the quote field specifically, I'm preferring `artifact.quote` over `artifact.text` when both exist, following the same pattern. The `surface` field matches exactly what I expect from propose.js, so no fallback is needed there since the detection-spec values are already valid.

I'm also considering edge cases: if both `artifact.quote` and `artifact.text` are present, the ternary correctly prioritizes `artifact.quote`. For `artifact.page_url`, I'm checking whether a `null` value versus `undefined` could cause issues with my fallback logic, but in practice propose.js never sets `artifact.page_url` at all, so it's genuinely `undefined` rather than `null`, making my `!== undefined` check safe.

To be more defensive against future changes where `page_url` might be explicitly `null`, I could switch to loose equality (`== null`) to treat both `null` and `undefined` as "not provided" and fall back to `candidate.page_url`. Since the ESLint config doesn't enforce the `eqeqeq` rule, using `==` here won't cause lint failures.

String: const VERIFIERS_BY_TYPE = {
  quote: verifyQuote,
  network_event: verifyNetworkEvent,
  register_row: verifyRegisterRow,
  coverage_proof: verifyCoverageProof,
};

// verifyCandidate(candidate, bundle) -> {verified, code, reason}. The fail-closed dispatcher: an
// unknown artifact.type is REJECTED, never passed through (Rule 4). Never mutates
// `candidate`.
function verifyCandidate(candidate, bundle) {
  if (!candidate || typeof candidate !== 'object') {
    return rejected(CODES.INVALID_CANDIDATE, 'candidate must be a non-null object');
  }
  const artifact = candidate.artifact;
  if (!artifact || typeof artifact !== 'object' || typeof artifact.type !== 'string' || !artifact.type) {
    return rejected(CODES.MISSING_ARTIFACT, 'candidate.artifact.type is required (Rule 3: no artifact, no breach)');
  }
  const verify = VERIFIERS_BY_TYPE[artifact.type];
  if (!verify) {
    return rejected(
      CODES.UNKNOWN_ARTIFACT_TYPE,
      'unrecognised artifact.type ' + JSON.stringify(artifact.type) + '; unknown artifact types fail closed, never pass through'
    );
  }
  return verify(artifact, bundle);
}</tool_use_error>

Deleted `breach/verifiers/.gitkeep` (redundant once the directory had real content).

### The normalisation rule, and why only whitespace

`normaliseWhitespace(s) = String(s).replace(/\s+/g, ' ')` — every run of whitespace collapses to one space; no trim, no case folding, no punctuation stripping. Applied identically to the candidate's quote and the bundle page's declared-surface text before an exact-substring check. Justification: `evidence/crawler/extract.js`'s `stripHtml()` turns block-level HTML boundaries into newlines, so a quote spanning a paragraph break would false-mismatch without this; nothing else is tolerated because that is precisely Gate 2's job (catch paraphrase/drift, per caution.md C-032).

### Rejection taxonomy (`CODES`, in `result.js`)

Closed set, one code per outcome: dispatch-level (`invalid_candidate`, `missing_artifact`, `unknown_artifact_type`), quote (`quote_verified`, `quote_missing_fields`, `quote_invalid_surface`, `quote_page_not_found`, `quote_surface_unavailable`, `quote_mismatch`), network_event (`*_verified`, `*_missing_fields`, `*_lane_not_run`, `*_not_found`), register_row (`*_verified`, `*_missing_fields`, `*_absent`, `*_mismatch`), coverage_proof (`*_verified`, `*_missing_fields`, `*_no_pages`, `*_pages_not_in_bundle`, `*_tier1_not_fetched`, `*_truncated`). Every result carries `{verified, code, reason}` — the exact `{verified, reason}` contract plus `code` for machine dispatch.

### Exit codes (all reproduced just now, this session)

- `node --test breach/verifiers/*.test.js`: **51/51 pass**.
- `node breach/verifiers/quote-match.js --calibrate`: **4/4 fixtures caught** (`quote_mismatch`, `network_event_not_found`, `register_row_mismatch`, `coverage_proof_truncated`).
- `node eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js --strict`: **green**, including `breach-artifact-rejected: 4 finding(s) across all 4 fixture(s) via breach/verifiers/quote-match.js` (another agent wired this into the central registry — I did not touch `run.js`).

### A confirmed bug found and fixed (in scope, additive)

While verifying, `breach/proposers/propose.js` landed concurrently (another Wave-2 builder). I wrote a throwaway probe (scratchpad only, not committed) piping real `propose()` output through my `verifyAll()` and found: `propose.js`'s `evalPresenceBreach` emits `{type:'quote', text, surface}` with `page_url` on the **candidate**, not the artifact — my originally-specified contract read `artifact.page_url`/`artifact.quote`. Every real quote candidate was being rejected (`quote_missing_fields`), 100% of the time. I fixed this with `resolveQuoteArtifact()` in `quote-match.js`: an artifact-level field always wins when present (my original contract still works, tested), and falls back to `candidate.page_url`/`artifact.text` only when the artifact-level field is absent. Confirmed fixed by re-running the probe (rejected→verified) and locked in with 4 new regression tests that hand-build the exact real shape (hermetic — they don't `require()` propose.js, so they can't break if that module's internals move again).

### Honest gaps (not fixed, need your call)

1. **`coverage_proof` and `register_row` do not match propose.js's real shape**, and I did not touch this. `propose.js`'s real absence-breach artifact is `{type:'coverage_proof', page_class, surface, pages_checked:[...], searched_patterns:[...]}` — no `coverage` sub-object, no `tier1_fetched`/`truncated` fields at all (propose.js's own `absenceInterlock()` already enforces truncation/footer/min-pages internally before emitting). My verifier currently rejects every real `coverage_proof` candidate with `coverage_proof_missing_fields`. Similarly, `propose.js`'s `evalRegister` emits `register_row` artifacts for the **no-match** case (`{type:'register_row', present:false, register, lane:'no_match', note}`), but my verifier is built to confirm a claimed **present** row matches the bundle — a fundamentally different check I didn't build. I did not redesign these myself because it's a genuine policy call (what should independently gate an absence claim, given propose.js already gates truncation internally) that affects real legal-safety guarantees, not a narrow field-rename like the quote fix. Recommend: either propose.js's artefact shapes are updated to match my original brief, or you give me (or whoever owns this) an explicit revised contract to build against.
2. **`network_event` from `brokenControlCandidate()`** emits `{type:'network_event', kind:'consent_control_broken', url, healthy}` — no `host`/`name` fields, so my `kind`+`host`+`name` identity check rejects it (`network_event_missing_fields`). The observed-entry path (`observedCandidates()`, spreading `bundle.browser.observed[]` entries directly) works correctly and is tested. The clean fix is probably propose.js reusing the observed-entry shape here too rather than reconstructing a fresh artifact from `consentControl`, but that's not my file.
3. **A second, unrelated vocabulary divergence exists between me and `breach/adjudicator/evidence-kind.js`** (also landed concurrently): its `OBSERVED_ARTIFACT_TYPES`/`REGISTER_ARTIFACT_TYPES`/`TEXT_ARTIFACT_TYPES` use different literal type strings (`network_request`, `cookie_jar_entry`, `corpus_quote`, `verbatim_quote`, `absence_claim`, etc.) than mine (`quote`, `network_event`, `register_row`, `coverage_proof`). This has not yet broken anything because `eval/e2e/lib/pipeline.js`'s `propose` stage currently only exercises `verifyAll` with real candidates from `propose.js` (which does agree with my `'quote'`/`'network_event'`/`'register_row'`/`'coverage_proof'` type names) — the adjudicator's differing vocabulary is a **downstream** concern once a `breach/adjudicator/index.js` composing entry point gets built. Flagging now so it's reconciled before that lands, not after.
